AND JOSHUA STECH'S DEMURRER TO THE FOURTH, FIFTH, AND SIXTH CAUSES OF ACTION OF PLAINTIFF'S FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT (FAC) IS SUSTAINED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND. THE COURT INSTRUCTS DEFENSE COUNSEL TO EMAIL A PROPOSED ORDER TO THE COURT. JUDGE: ETHAN P. SCHULMAN; CLERK: SEAN KANE; COURT REPORTER: MARIA A. WHEN THE COURT RESUMES NORMAL OPERATIONS, THE PRESIDING JUDGE WILL ANNOUNCE PROCEDURES FOR SETTING HEARING DATES FOR THOSE CASES WHICH HAVE BEEN FULLY BRIEFED. DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-23-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES: Atty. JAMIE D. WELLS, Esq. of McGuireWoods (Tel: 415-844-1972) appearing via CourtCall for plaintiff LendingHome Corporation;
Atty. BRADFORD NEWMAN of Baker & McKenzie LLP (Tel: 650-856-2400) appearing via CourtCall for defendants Sundae, Inc. and Joshua Stech.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT JOSHUA STECH, SUNDAE, INC. DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT

Defendants Sundae, Inc. and Joshua Stech's demurrer to the fourth, fifth, and sixth causes of action of plaintiff's first amended complaint (FAC) is sustained without leave to amend. Plaintiff's causes of action for intentional interference with contractual relations, aiding and abetting, and UCL claim are all displaced by California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). (See Zengen, Inc. v. Comerica Bank (2007) 41 Cal.4th 239, 247, fn. 5 [the California Supreme Court has criticized the term "preempt," preferring the term "displace"].)

California's version of the UTSA displaces common law claims that are "based on the same nucleus of facts as the misappropriation of trade secrets claim for relief." (K.C. Multimedia, Inc. v. Bank of America Tech, & Operations, Inc. (2009) 171 Cal.App.4th 939, 954-95; see also, e.g., Silvaco Data Systems v. Intel Corp. (2010) 184 Cal.App.4th 210, 232-236, disapproved on other grounds, Kwikset Corp. v. Superior Court (2011) 51 Cal.4th 301, 337 [holding that CUTSA "preempts any other statutory or common law claim predicated on the theft of a trade secret," including claims for conversion, common counts, common law unfair business practices, and intentional and negligent misrepresentation].) CUTSA also "bars UCL claims sounding in misappropriation of trade secrets." (Silvaco Data Systems, 184 Cal.App.4th at 241; see also, e.g., Deerpoint Group, Inc. v. Agrigenix, LLC (E.D. Cal. 2018) 345 F.Supp.3d 1207, 1238 [to the extent that UCL claim relied on trade secret misappropriation, it was preempted by CUTSA]; K.C. Multimedia, Inc., 171 Cal.App.4th at 960-962 [holding that a UCL claim and interference with contract claim that were both based on the same nucleus of operative facts as a CUTSA claim were preempted].)

Here, the FAC as a whole rests on factual allegations of trade secret misappropriation. The "conduct at the heart of [plaintiff's] claim[s]" (K.C. Multimedia, Inc., 171 Cal.App.4th at 960) is that defendants used plaintiff's confidential information and trade secrets for defendants' benefit and to plaintiff's detriment to form defendant's company, Sundae Inc. (FAC 70, 83, 105.) Whether defendants themselves improperly acquired plaintiff's trade secrets, or whether defendants used former employees to acquire these trade secrets and confidential information is immaterial. The FAC alleges defendants solicited plaintiff's employees to work for Sundae while still being employed at LendingHome. (FAC 68.) These former employees improperly acquired, used, and/or disclosed plaintiff's trade secrets and/or proprietary information prior to their departure. (FAC 69.) The fourth, fifth, and sixth causes of action all rest on factual allegations of trade secret misappropriation. (See FAC 101-105, 108, 116, 123.) These allegations stem directly from the gravamen of plaintiff's complaint, that defendants misappropriated plaintiff's confidential information and trade secrets for defendants' benefit.

The Court instructs defense counsel to email a proposed order to the Court.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/EPS)
